title: machine: add ability to insert new disks to the VM
body:
We can now add more disks to the podman machine with the following flags:

 *--extra-disk-num

Number of extra disk(s) to add to the guest VM.
Can only be increased. Only supported for QEMU machines.

*--extra-disk-size
Size of the extra disk(s) for the guest VM in GB.
Cannot be changed on already added disk. Only supported for QEMU machines.

The `init`, `set`, `rm`, `inspect` CLI arguments have been implemented. Can be run like so:

```
podman machine init myvm --extra-disk-num 3 --extra-disk-size 50
```

Right now, the default size is the same as the default root disk. I need to update the https://github.com/containers/common repo.

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]". That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
The podman-machine utility has gained the ability to attach additional disks to the virtual machine. The number and the size of the extra added disks are configurable. The support is limited to QEMU.
```

You need to document the new options in the man pages.

@ashley-cui @baude PTAL

> What is the use case for this? @baude @ashley-cui PTAL

The use case is running containers inside the VM with attached storage. For instance, storage components like [Stratis](https://stratis-storage.github.io/) or [Ceph](https://ceph.io/en/) need access to block devices to operate.

> You need to document the new options in the man pages.
> 
> @ashley-cui @baude PTAL

I already updated `docs/source/markdown`, did I miss something?

> The use case is running containers inside the VM with attached storage. For instance, storage components like [Stratis](https://stratis-storage.github.io/) or [Ceph](https://ceph.io/en/) need access to block devices to operate.

But why would anyone want to do this? Podman machine is to run podman in a VM and have a simple setup, I do not see the use case of using either stratis or ceph inside the VM? What is the end goal here?

Given that on qemu, the primary disk can be expanded after fact combined with what @Luap99 said, I'm not connecting the dots on why this is needed.  @leseb could you perhaps verbosely describe your use case?

> > The use case is running containers inside the VM with attached storage. For instance, storage components like [Stratis](https://stratis-storage.github.io/) or [Ceph](https://ceph.io/en/) need access to block devices to operate.
> 
> But why would anyone want to do this? Podman machine is to run podman in a VM and have a simple setup, I do not see the use case of using either stratis or ceph inside the VM? What is the end goal here?

CI testing, local development environment, and demos are use cases. podman-machine is great to provide an isolation from the host since it's a VM. If I decide to test a small containerized scenario to deploy Ceph or Stratis this code is really useful. I can dynamically allocate as many drives as possible, something that is not possible if I don't use the VM. Not all technologies accept loop devices and working with "real" devices gets you closer to something you might get in production.
Hope this helps.

> Given that on qemu, the primary disk can be expanded after fact combined with what @Luap99 said, I'm not connecting the dots on why this is needed. @leseb could you perhaps verbosely describe your use case?

Expanding the primary disk is not enough since you only get more space on the root disk but you only have a single filesystem. If you need block devices this solution does not help, unfortunately. Hope this clarifies.

The problem I see with this it is very small use case and only useful for testing. As I said podman machine is not a general purpose VM manager, there are existing solutions on the market for this.

The problem is that we need to maintain these things, there is work to add hyper V and native apple hypervisor backends, making sure options work on them adds work for us. Sure it is simple to say it only works with qemu but eventually somebody is going to want that for another backend.

At some point we have to draw a line what we want to support with machine and I think the line is here.
i also believe this can be accomplished manually by editing the JSON file @leseb ... want me to try that and report back ?

@Luap99 It's not a small use case if you work on storage or database systems 😉. I understand the rationale and the desire to keep the feature set under control though. You mentioned that the use case is only useful for testing, can you explain to me the scope of podman machine? How are we expecting users to use it? Thanks!

@baude yes please 😃, but I assume one needs to populate/delete the disks "manually", right?

PR needs rebase.
